methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Late Stage → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.2x) to $5.7B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Transportation (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 2 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Transportation, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
The Boring Company is a late-stage Transportation sector venture that has raised $675M to date and carries a last post-money valuation of $5.7B. The peer universe in Transportation is limited, consisting of four private comparables (Rivian, Einride, Harbinger, REGENT Craft) with no public traded peers identified. The narrow peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage datapoints to support a reliable median-based valuation, precluding peer-multiple triangulation.

Stage-progression methodology is applicable and forms the primary valuation signal. Applied to the $5.7B last post-money with a 1.2x step-up multiplier typical for Late Stage progression, this method yields $6.8B. The 1.2x multiplier reflects conventional step-up magnitude between late-stage and subsequent rounds absent company-specific acceleration factors or material deterioration in market conditions.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $5.8B (low) to $7.9B (median $6.8B), derived from the single applicable method. The wide range reflects Stage-progression alone cannot fully constrain valuation; absent a sufficiently populated peer-median check or alternative triangulation anchors, material uncertainty persists around fair value within this band. Key risks include execution on capital-intensive tunnel infrastructure, regulatory approval timelines, and the ability to achieve unit economics at commercial scale—factors not quantitatively embedded in stage-based mechanics.
